glossary.def: The square root of the mean of the squared residuals. Reported in the target's own units and dominated by large errors because squaring inflates them, so it is the metric to choose when big misses are disproportionately costly. RMSE is always greater than or equal to MAE.

The mean of the absolute residuals. It punishes every unit of error equally and is far more robust to outliers than RMSE; the estimator that minimizes it is the conditional median, so MAE-tuned models favor the typical case.

The average absolute error expressed as a percentage of the true value, which makes error comparable across series of different scale. It explodes when any true value is near zero and is asymmetric, biasing forecasts low, so it must never be used on data containing zeros.

The 2x2 table of true positives, false positives, false negatives, and true negatives produced by thresholding a classifier. Every classification metric is a ratio of these four counts, and the threshold trades false alarms against misses.

Precision is the fraction of predicted positives that are truly positive (TP/(TP+FP)); recall is the fraction of true positives that are caught (TP/(TP+FN)). Precision matters when false alarms are costly, recall when misses are costly, and the threshold buys one at the expense of the other.

The harmonic mean of precision and recall. Because the harmonic mean is dragged toward the smaller value, F1 is high only when precision and recall are both high; it is the standard summary metric on imbalanced data where accuracy is misleading.

On imbalanced data, a model that always predicts the majority class can score very high accuracy while catching no minority cases, so accuracy looks excellent yet the model is useless. This is why accuracy should never be reported alone on imbalanced problems.

A proper scoring rule that grades predicted probabilities, not just labels: it penalizes each row by minus the log of the probability assigned to the true class. The penalty is zero for a confident-correct prediction and unbounded for a confident-wrong one, so it rewards confidence only when earned.

Gneiting, T. & Raftery, A. E. (2007). Strictly Proper Scoring Rules, Prediction, and Estimation. J. American Statistical Association 102(477).
Hyndman, R. J. & Koehler, A. B. (2006). Another Look at Measures of Forecast Accuracy. International J. Forecasting 22(4).
Chicco, D. & Jurman, G. (2020). The advantages of the Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) over F1 score and accuracy in binary classification evaluation. BMC Genomics 21:6.
